purpose: Binary Yes/No decisions for each canonical sector based on direct regulated addressees, products, facilities, installations, or unmistakably sector-bound activities.

rules: Answer every sector as Yes or No.
Emit only sectors answered Yes in final_applicable_sectors.
All Industries is not a default; answer Yes only for truly cross-sector duties and when no specific sector dominates.
Public Sector requires public bodies to be substantive regulated actors, not mere implementers, regulators, transposers, or enforcers.
Specific sectors override broad Manufacturing, Construction, Energy, Public Sector, or All Industries unless those broad sectors have separate direct evidence.
Do not infer sectors from indirect economic impact or from final SpecificTopic labels alone.

tag_decisions.nearby_label_if_any: optional sector that better fits the evidence

final_applicable_sectors: all sectors from tag_decisions with answer=Yes, after sector reconciliation

tags.definition: Broad cross-sector obligations applying to undertakings, employers, operators, producers, traders, controllers, entities, or organizations regardless of industry.
tags.decision_question: Should this source be tagged ApplicableSectors = "All Industries (Sector-Agnostic)"?
yes_when: The source applies generally across sectors or to broad actor classes and no direct sector-bound product, operator, facility, or activity dominates.
There is a source-supported evidence role: regulated_object, legal_mechanism, protected_interest, direct_addressee, direct_regulated_product/facility, or final-topic ESRS mapping trace.
The tag is primary, companion, or benchmark_expected_companion; it is not merely adjacent or incidental.
no_when: Do not use merely because Member States or authorities implement the law. Do not use when one or more specific sectors are the clear regulated addressees.
The only support is a keyword hit, source URL/domain/slug, filename, row ID, benchmark answer, generic legal boilerplate, or implementation/enforcement actor.
A narrower or different canonical label captures the operative source evidence more accurately.

test_case.question: A source's title/scope/operative provisions centrally say: "The producer shall be liable for damage caused by a defect in his product.". Should the LLM tag ApplicableSectors = "All Industries (Sector-Agnostic)"? Answer Yes/No and cite the evidence role.
test_case.reasoning_check: Yes if the quoted evidence satisfies the tag_when rule: The source applies generally across sectors or to broad actor classes and no direct sector-bound product, operator, facility, or activity dominates.
forces_reasoning_about: whether the excerpt is source evidence rather than a benchmark artifact

test_case.question: A source mentions "all undertakings" once in background, but its operative duties regulate a different subject and no duty matches "The source applies generally across sectors or to broad actor classes and no direct sector-bound product, operator, facility, or activity dominates.". Should the LLM tag ApplicableSectors = "All Industries (Sector-Agnostic)"?
test_case.reasoning_check: No. A keyword or background reference is not enough; the label needs source-supported central evidence.

test_case.question: A source fits this exclusion: "Do not use merely because Member States or authorities implement the law. Do not use when one or more specific sectors are the clear regulated addressees.". Should the LLM tag ApplicableSectors = "All Industries (Sector-Agnostic)"?
test_case.reasoning_check: No. The label-specific do_not_tag_when rule excludes this case.

test_case.question: The master evidence includes a weak/indirect edge example: "This Directive concerns the collection, treatment and discharge of urban waste water and the treatment and discharge of waste water from certain industrial sectors.". Standing alone, should the LLM tag ApplicableSectors = "All Industries (Sector-Agnostic)"?
test_case.reasoning_check: The master reason was classified as weak/cautionary; the LLM must look for central legal duties, not rely on weak contextual association.
